questions.subject: ap-comparative-government

No — fixed term, no confidence vote
President directly elected; PM nominated by president

questions.question: According to the table, which of the following best distinguishes parliamentary systems from presidential systems?

choices.text: Parliamentary systems can remove the executive via a no-confidence vote; presidential systems lack this — executives serve fixed terms instead.
choices.explanation: The table confirms that the UK (parliamentary) removes the PM via a no-confidence vote, while Mexico and Nigeria (presidential) cannot remove the executive this way — matching PAU-3.A.1 and PAU-3.A.2.

choices.text: Presidential systems always feature appointed prime ministers, while parliamentary systems rely on direct presidential elections.
choices.explanation: The table shows the opposite: parliamentary systems have PMs drawn from the legislature (not directly elected), while presidential systems elect the president directly — the choice misidentifies which system uses which selection method.

choices.text: Parliamentary systems feature constitutionally fixed executive terms, which makes them structurally more stable and predictable than presidential systems.
choices.explanation: Fixed executive terms are a feature of presidential systems, not parliamentary systems; a parliamentary PM can be removed at any time through a no-confidence vote, making tenure less fixed.

choices.text: Semi-presidential systems are identical to presidential systems because both feature a directly elected head of state.
choices.explanation: The table distinguishes semi-presidential Russia from presidential Mexico and Nigeria: Russia has both a directly elected president AND a PM accountable to the legislature — a dual structure absent from pure presidential systems.

questions.subject: ap-comparative-government
questions.question: Which of the following countries is the College Board's primary example of a semi-presidential system?

choices.text: United Kingdom
choices.explanation: The United Kingdom is the CED's primary example of a parliamentary system, not a semi-presidential one; the PM is drawn from and accountable to Parliament.

choices.text: Mexico
choices.explanation: Mexico is a presidential system in which the elected president serves as both head of state and head of government with a fixed term — there is no separately accountable prime minister.

choices.text: Russia
choices.explanation: Per PAU-3.A.3, Russia is the CED's named example of a semi-presidential system: the president is directly elected, and the president nominates a prime minister who must be approved by the State Duma.

choices.text: China
choices.explanation: China does not fit any of the three system types neatly; it is a one-party state in which the CPC controls government; the premier is the head of government but is not accountable to a competitive legislature.

questions.subject: ap-comparative-government

stimulus.content: "In Country X, the prime minister and cabinet must maintain the continuous support of the national legislature. If a majority of legislators withdraw that support, the government falls and new elections may be called. The prime minister is not separately elected by the public but instead leads the party or coalition with the most seats in the legislature."

questions.question: Country X is best described as which of the following types of political system?

choices.text: Presidential system
choices.explanation: In a presidential system the executive is separately elected and cannot be removed by a legislative no-confidence vote; the passage describes the opposite arrangement.

choices.text: Parliamentary system
choices.explanation: The passage describes the defining features of a parliamentary system per PAU-3.A.1: the executive is drawn from the legislature, cabinet accountability is continuous, and removal is by no-confidence vote — exactly the UK model.

choices.text: Semi-presidential system
choices.explanation: A semi-presidential system features a separately elected president in addition to a PM accountable to the legislature; Country X has no separately elected president — it is fully parliamentary.

choices.text: Theocratic system
choices.explanation: A theocratic system grounds authority in religious doctrine, with clerical leaders holding ultimate power; the passage describes secular legislative accountability mechanisms, not religious governance.

questions.subject: ap-comparative-government
questions.question: Which of the following best describes the role of the UK monarch in the political system?

choices.text: The monarch serves as head of government, directing the civil service and setting foreign policy.
choices.explanation: In the UK it is the prime minister, not the monarch, who directs the civil service and sets foreign policy; the monarch's governmental role is ceremonial.

choices.text: The monarch is a ceremonial head of state who formally appoints the leader of the Commons majority as prime minister.
choices.explanation: Per PAU-3.C.2.f, the UK monarch is a ceremonial head of state who formally appoints the PM — exercising no independent political discretion in modern practice.

choices.text: The monarch can dissolve Parliament and call new elections at any time without ministerial advice.
choices.explanation: Royal prerogative powers are exercised on the advice of ministers; the monarch acts on convention, not independent political will — unilateral dissolution without ministerial advice is constitutionally inappropriate.

choices.text: The monarch independently commands the UK's armed forces and makes key military decisions without requiring the prime minister's advice.
choices.explanation: Although the monarch is formally commander in chief, in practice the prime minister exercises de facto command over the armed forces; the monarch's military role is ceremonial, not operational.

questions.subject: ap-comparative-government

stimulus.content: "A political scientist comparing legislative structures notes: two of the six AP Comparative Government course countries have unicameral national legislatures, while the other four have bicameral legislatures with distinct upper and lower chambers."

questions.question: Which of the following correctly identifies the two countries with unicameral legislatures?

choices.text: Mexico and Nigeria
choices.explanation: Both Mexico and Nigeria have bicameral legislatures — a Chamber of Deputies and Senate in Mexico, and a Senate and House of Representatives in Nigeria.

choices.text: China and Iran
choices.explanation: Per PAU-3.E.1.a-b, China's National People's Congress (NPC) and Iran's Majles are both unicameral national legislatures — the two course examples of single-chamber systems.

choices.text: Russia and China
choices.explanation: Russia has a bicameral legislature — the State Duma (lower) and the Federation Council (upper); only China, not Russia, has a unicameral legislature among course countries.

choices.text: United Kingdom and Iran
choices.explanation: The United Kingdom has a bicameral legislature — the House of Commons and the House of Lords; only Iran, not the UK, has a unicameral legislature.

questions.subject: ap-comparative-government
questions.question: Which of the following best describes the Politburo Standing Committee in China?

choices.text: The popularly elected legislative body that formally approves legislation, confirms the premier, and nominates the president.
choices.explanation: That description fits the National People's Congress (NPC); the Politburo Standing Committee is a small CPC inner circle that is not popularly elected.

choices.text: The CPC's inner circle that holds real governing authority, making decisions the NPC subsequently ratifies.
choices.explanation: Per PAU-3.F.1.a, the Politburo Standing Committee is the actual center of power in the Chinese state; it makes governing decisions that the NPC subsequently ratifies.

choices.text: China's upper legislative chamber, which reviews and amends bills passed by the NPC.
choices.explanation: China has a unicameral legislature; there is no upper chamber; the Standing Committee of the NPC (a different body) handles legislative duties between NPC sessions, but neither is the Politburo Standing Committee.

choices.text: An advisory judicial body that reviews legislation for constitutional compliance.
choices.explanation: China has no independent constitutional court; the Politburo Standing Committee is a political leadership body within the CPC, not a judicial or quasi-judicial institution.

questions.subject: ap-comparative-government

stimulus.content: "Mexico's president serves one six-year term and may not seek re-election under any circumstances. Once the term concludes, the officeholder must leave office regardless of the political situation or the continuity of ongoing policy programs."

questions.question: Which of the following is a recognized advantage of Mexico's single-term presidential limit?

choices.text: It ensures experienced leaders remain in office long enough to achieve their policy goals.
choices.explanation: A single non-renewable term limits time in office and may prevent leaders from fully achieving goals — this is a recognized disadvantage of term limits, not an advantage.

choices.text: It eliminates the lame-duck period that weakens executive authority near the end of a term.
choices.explanation: The single non-renewable term actually creates a lame-duck dynamic throughout the final phase of the term; it does not eliminate the lame-duck problem — it may intensify it.

choices.text: It checks executive power and inhibits the rise of personality-based authoritarian rule.
choices.explanation: Per PAU-3.C.3a, a recognized advantage of executive term limits is that they check executive power and inhibit personality-driven authoritarian consolidation by forcing periodic leadership change.

choices.text: It promotes long-term policy continuity by preventing abrupt and disruptive shifts in the new government's priorities.
choices.explanation: Mandatory single-term limits can disrupt policy continuity because each new president may bring different priorities; the CED lists policy discontinuity as a disadvantage of term limits.

questions.subject: ap-comparative-government
questions.question: Which of the following correctly identifies both chambers of Russia's bicameral legislature and their primary selection methods?

choices.text: State Duma (elected) and Federation Council (appointed).
choices.explanation: Per PAU-3.E.1.e, the State Duma is elected by citizens, while the Federation Council is appointed by regional governors and legislatures — making it the appointed upper house.

choices.text: The State Duma (appointed by regional authorities) and the Federation Council (elected by popular vote).
choices.explanation: This reverses the correct arrangement; the State Duma is the elected lower house, and the Federation Council is the appointed upper house — not the other way around.

choices.text: The Majles (elected) and the Guardian Council (appointed).
choices.explanation: The Majles and Guardian Council are Iranian institutions, not Russian; Russia's legislature consists of the State Duma and the Federation Council.

choices.text: The Chamber of Deputies (elected) and the Senate (elected).
choices.explanation: The Chamber of Deputies and Senate are Mexico's legislative chambers, not Russia's; Russia's lower house is the State Duma and its upper house is the Federation Council.

questions.subject: ap-comparative-government

stimulus.content: "Despite holding regular sessions, Country X's national legislature has never voted down an executive proposal. Observers note that the legislature convenes only once a year for a brief period, during which it ratifies decisions that have already been made by a small committee of the ruling party. Opposition within the legislature is absent, and all major policy decisions are announced before the legislative session begins."

questions.question: Country X's legislature is best described by which of the following concepts?

choices.text: An independent legislature that reinforces democratic legitimacy by openly debating policy.
choices.explanation: The passage shows no open debate and no independent rejection of proposals; per PAU-3.F.2, independent legislatures respond to public demand and debate policy openly — none of which describes Country X.

choices.text: A rubber-stamp legislature that legitimizes decisions already made by the ruling party.
choices.explanation: Country X mirrors China's NPC: per PAU-3.E.1.a, the NPC constitutionally holds supreme authority but in practice meets briefly to ratify decisions already made by the Politburo Standing Committee — the definition of a rubber-stamp legislature.

choices.text: A bicameral legislature in which the upper chamber delays lower-house legislation.
choices.explanation: The passage describes a single body with no mention of two chambers; the dynamic described is party dominance over a legislative body, not an inter-chamber delay mechanism.

choices.text: A theocratic legislature in which religious scholars review proposed laws.
choices.explanation: Country X's legislature is constrained by a party committee, not by religious scholars; this describes Iran's Guardian Council oversight dynamic, not the scenario in the passage.

questions.subject: ap-comparative-government

stimulus.content: "Iran's parliament has passed several economic reform bills aimed at reducing import restrictions. However, before any legislation takes effect, a twelve-member body reviews the bills to determine whether they comply with Islamic Sharia law and the country's constitution. Six members of this body are Islamic jurists appointed by the Supreme Leader; the other six are civil lawyers nominated by the head of the judiciary and approved by parliament."

questions.question: The body described in the passage performs which of the following functions?

choices.text: It serves as the upper house of Iran's bicameral legislature, amending bills before they reach the president.
choices.explanation: Iran's Majles is unicameral; the body described is not a legislative chamber but a review body that vets legislation for compliance with Islamic law — the Guardian Council.

choices.text: It resolves disputes between the Majles and other institutions by serving as an advisory committee to the Supreme Leader.
choices.explanation: That describes the Expediency Council, which resolves disputes between the Majles and the Guardian Council; the passage describes a 12-member body that reviews legislation for Sharia compliance — a different function.

choices.text: It constrains legislative independence by vetting laws for compatibility with Islamic law and the constitution.
choices.explanation: Per PAU-3.F.1.d, Iran's Guardian Council (six jurists + six civil lawyers) vets candidates and oversees the Majles to ensure laws comply with Islamic law — exactly the function described in the passage.

choices.text: It selects, oversees, and can remove the Supreme Leader if the leader loses the confidence of Islamic scholars.
choices.explanation: That function belongs to Iran's Assembly of Experts; the Guardian Council reviews legislation for Sharia compliance and vets candidates — it does not have the power to remove the Supreme Leader.

questions.subject: ap-comparative-government

stimulus.content: "A parliamentary government faces a political crisis after a controversial minister's remarks inflame public debate. Legislators from opposition parties submit a formal motion expressing disapproval of the minister's conduct. The motion passes with a slim majority, but the prime minister refuses to dismiss the minister, arguing the rebuke carries no binding legal force."

questions.question: The motion described in the passage is best understood as which of the following parliamentary mechanisms?

choices.text: A vote of no confidence that automatically removes the prime minister from office.
choices.explanation: A vote of no confidence targets the government as a whole and, if passed successfully, requires the PM and cabinet to resign; the passage describes a narrower rebuke of one minister that is non-binding — a censure, not a no-confidence vote.

choices.text: Formal impeachment proceedings in which a two-thirds legislative supermajority is required to remove the minister from office.
choices.explanation: Impeachment is a different mechanism used in presidential systems (e.g., Nigeria) to remove executives; the passage describes a parliamentary rebuke that the PM says carries no binding force — characteristic of censure, not impeachment.

choices.text: A censure — a formal parliamentary rebuke expressing legislative disapproval without legally compelling the minister's removal.
choices.explanation: Per PAU-3.B.2, parliaments may censure cabinet ministers as a check on the executive; censure expresses disapproval but does not legally compel removal, matching the PM's response in the passage.

choices.text: A Question Time session during which legislators interrogate the minister directly.
choices.explanation: Question Time is a practice of oral questioning, not a formal motion expressing disapproval; the passage describes a motion that is voted upon and passed — a censure proceeding, not an interrogation session.

questions.subject: ap-comparative-government
questions.question: Which of the following conclusions is best supported by the data in the table?

choices.text: Countries in which the head of state and head of government are the same person always have presidential systems.
choices.explanation: While Mexico and Nigeria fuse the two roles and are presidential systems, China formally separates them yet is neither presidential nor parliamentary — the table shows the pattern is not a perfect rule.

choices.text: Presidential systems tend to fuse head of state and head of government in one elected official.
choices.explanation: Per PAU-3.A.2, in presidential systems the top executive serves as both head of state and head of government; Mexico and Nigeria both confirm this pattern, as shown in the table.

choices.text: Any country that includes a prime minister in its governmental structure must be classified as a parliamentary system.
choices.explanation: Russia and Iran both have prime ministers yet are not parliamentary systems (Russia is semi-presidential; Iran is a theocratic hybrid); having a prime minister does not define a parliamentary system.

choices.text: Iran's Supreme Leader and elected president share equal governing authority.
choices.explanation: The table distinguishes the two roles, and per PAU-3.C.2.b, the Supreme Leader holds far greater authority than the elected president — commanding the military, appointing the judiciary, and setting the political agenda.

questions.subject: ap-comparative-government

stimulus.content: "In the autumn session of Country Y's parliament, the opposition leader rose to ask the prime minister to explain why the government had awarded a large infrastructure contract without competitive bidding. The prime minister responded publicly on the floor of the house, defending the decision and committing to a full audit. Two weeks later, after a legislative committee found procedural errors, the opposition tabled a motion requiring a vote on whether the government still commanded the confidence of parliament."

questions.question: The events in the passage best illustrate which feature of parliamentary systems?

choices.text: The legislature can remove the executive without a formal vote if the prime minister loses public support.
choices.explanation: Parliamentary removal requires a formal no-confidence vote, not simply declining public approval; the passage in fact describes a structured process — questions, committee review, then a formal motion — not informal removal.

choices.text: Parliamentary systems check the executive through question time, committee oversight, and the threat of a no-confidence vote.
choices.explanation: Per PAU-3.B.1 and PAU-3.B.2, parliamentary systems have multiple checks despite fused powers: oral questions, legislative committees, and the threat of no-confidence proceedings — all illustrated in the passage.

choices.text: Parliamentary systems give the executive unchecked power because the PM controls the legislative majority.
choices.explanation: This is the misconception identified in §5 of unit-2.md; the passage shows meaningful checks — the opposition questions the PM, committees investigate, and a no-confidence motion is tabled.

choices.text: In parliamentary systems, the judiciary routinely intervenes to resolve constitutional disputes arising between the executive and the legislature.
choices.explanation: The passage describes legislative mechanisms — questions, committee review, and a confidence vote — not judicial intervention; UK parliamentary tradition generally resolves executive-legislative disputes through internal parliamentary procedures.

questions.subject: ap-comparative-government

stimulus.content: "Nigeria's National Assembly voted to begin impeachment proceedings against the president following allegations of gross misconduct. Under the Nigerian constitution, removal requires both chambers to pass a resolution by a two-thirds majority after a panel investigates the charges. If upheld, the vice president assumes the presidency."

questions.question: The process described in the passage reflects which of the following features of Nigeria's political system?

choices.text: Nigeria's parliament holds the power to remove the president through a simple majority no-confidence vote, which can be triggered at any time.
choices.explanation: Nigeria's presidential system does not use a vote of no confidence; removal requires impeachment by a two-thirds majority in both chambers for gross misconduct — a much higher threshold than a simple majority confidence vote.

choices.text: Nigeria's presidential system allows impeachment by a two-thirds vote in both chambers — a legal mechanism for removal that has rarely been invoked.
choices.explanation: Per PAU-3.D.1, Nigeria's National Assembly (both chambers by two-thirds majority) can impeach the president for gross misconduct — a difficult but legally available removal mechanism in a presidential system.

choices.text: Nigeria's president can only be removed by a vote within the ruling political party, not by the legislature.
choices.explanation: Party-internal removal is a feature of authoritarian systems like China's; Nigeria's constitution vests impeachment power in the elected National Assembly, not in a political party.

choices.text: Nigeria's semi-presidential system allows the prime minister to dismiss the president when misconduct is alleged.
choices.explanation: Nigeria is a presidential, not semi-presidential, system; it has no prime minister; the president is both head of state and head of government, and removal is by legislative impeachment.

questions.subject: ap-comparative-government

stimulus.content: "In Country Z, the Supreme Leader commands the armed forces, appoints the head of the judiciary, oversees the vetting of candidates for elected office, and sets the country's broad political agenda. A separately elected president manages the civil service and conducts day-to-day foreign policy. The Supreme Leader serves indefinitely and can only be removed by an assembly of senior religious scholars."

questions.question: Country Z's institutional structure is most consistent with which of the following course country examples?

choices.text: Russia, which features both a powerful elected president and an appointed prime minister who share executive authority across separate policy domains.
choices.explanation: Russia's dual executive consists of an elected president (dominant) and an appointed prime minister — not an unelected Supreme Leader with a separately elected president; Russia lacks a theocratic vetting authority.

choices.text: China, in which the CPC General Secretary exercises authority above the formally elected institutions.
choices.explanation: China's CPC General Secretary and state president are now the same person; China has no elected president separate from its paramount leader — the passage's elected president alongside an unelected Supreme Leader fits Iran, not China.

choices.text: Iran, where the Supreme Leader holds paramount authority above the elected president and is only removable by the Assembly of Experts.
choices.explanation: Per PAU-3.C.2.b, Iran's Supreme Leader commands the military, appoints the judiciary, oversees the Guardian Council's candidate vetting, and serves indefinitely — the Assembly of Experts holds the sole removal power, matching Country Z exactly.

choices.text: United Kingdom, in which the monarch holds supreme authority above an elected prime minister.
choices.explanation: The UK monarch is a ceremonial head of state with no real political authority; in practice the PM governs; the monarch's powers are conventional and do not compare to the Supreme Leader's commanding authority in Iran.

questions.subject: ap-comparative-government

stimulus.content: "An analyst writing about judicial systems observes: 'In Country A, judges issue rulings that have repeatedly overturned executive orders as unconstitutional — even when the ruling party disagreed with the outcome. Judges serve 15-year non-renewable terms and are removed only through formal impeachment procedures. In Country B, courts formally have the power of judicial review, but their decisions consistently favor the executive, and opposition figures receive harsher sentences regardless of evidence.'"

questions.question: Which of the following best explains the difference between Country A and Country B described in the passage?

choices.text: Country A has a bicameral legislature while Country B has a unicameral legislature.
choices.explanation: The distinction the passage draws is about judicial independence, not legislative structure; bicameralism vs. unicameralism is irrelevant to the judicial patterns described.

choices.text: Country A operates under the principle of rule of law, while Country B operates under rule by law.
choices.explanation: Per PAU-3.G.1.a and PAU-3.G.1.i, rule of law means courts constrain the government (Country A); rule by law means the government uses courts as instruments of control (Country B) — the classic China-vs-UK distinction applied here.

choices.text: Country A uses a common law system while Country B uses Sharia law.
choices.explanation: The passage describes a judicial independence difference, not a legal tradition difference; rule by law applies to China's CPC-controlled judiciary, which is not based on Sharia law.

choices.text: Country A's judiciary is appointed by the Supreme Leader, giving it independence; Country B's is elected, making it politically accountable.
choices.explanation: Appointment by a supreme leader does not produce judicial independence — Iran's Supreme Leader-appointed judiciary serves the theocratic agenda; the passage's Country A has 15-year terms and formal review power, suggesting structural independence.

questions.subject: ap-comparative-government

stimulus.content: "Scholars studying term limits note that when an executive cannot be re-elected, they may push through ambitious — sometimes risky — reforms without fully accounting for long-term consequences. Additionally, the final months of such an executive's tenure are often characterized by reduced bargaining leverage with other political actors, who anticipate the executive's imminent departure."

questions.question: The passage most directly supports which of the following arguments about executive term limits?

choices.text: Term limits always strengthen democratic governance by ensuring regular leadership change.
choices.explanation: The CED presents term limits as having both advantages and disadvantages; the passage describes two disadvantages (poorly designed policy and lame-duck weakness) — not a blanket endorsement of term limits.

choices.text: Term limits can produce disadvantages including poorly designed policy and a lame-duck period that weakens the executive.
choices.explanation: Per PAU-3.C.3b, CED-listed disadvantages of term limits include poorly designed policy and the lame-duck period; the passage describes both — hurried reform decisions and reduced bargaining leverage near term's end.

choices.text: Term-limited executives are more likely to be impeached than executives without term limits.
choices.explanation: The passage makes no claim about impeachment rates; it addresses policy quality and political leverage — both recognized disadvantages of term limits in the CED, not legal vulnerability to removal.

choices.text: The advantages of term limits — such as preventing dictatorship — always outweigh the disadvantages described in the passage.
choices.explanation: The CED explicitly presents term limits as having both advantages and disadvantages without declaring one set superior; the passage focuses on the disadvantages, and the 'always outweigh' claim is not supported.

questions.subject: ap-comparative-government
questions.question: Which of the following best describes the function of Iran's Expediency Council?

choices.text: It vets candidates for elected office to ensure they meet Islamic and constitutional qualifications.
choices.explanation: Candidate vetting is the function of the Guardian Council, not the Expediency Council; the Expediency Council resolves institutional disputes.

choices.text: It selects and can remove the Supreme Leader based on the judgment of senior Islamic scholars.
choices.explanation: The power to select and remove the Supreme Leader belongs to the Assembly of Experts; the Expediency Council has a mediation function, not a personnel function over the Supreme Leader.

choices.text: It resolves disputes between the Majles and the Guardian Council and advises the Supreme Leader.
choices.explanation: Per PAU-3.F.1.c, the Expediency Council is appointed by the Supreme Leader to mediate conflicts between the Majles and the Guardian Council and to serve as an advisory body to the Supreme Leader.

choices.text: It approves the national budget and confirms cabinet ministers nominated by the president.
choices.explanation: Approving the budget and confirming cabinet nominees are powers of the Majles (Iran's elected parliament), not the Expediency Council.

questions.subject: ap-comparative-government

stimulus.content: "The House of Commons passed a motion of no confidence in the prime minister's government by a margin of twelve votes. The prime minister immediately announced that the government would resign, and the monarch invited the leader of the largest opposition party to attempt to form a new government. Within two weeks, a new prime minister had been sworn in without a general election."

questions.question: Which of the following best explains why the process described is possible in the United Kingdom but would not be applicable in Mexico?

choices.text: The UK allows its monarch to make all major executive appointments, while Mexico's president is constrained by judicial review.
choices.explanation: The passage's key mechanism is legislative removal of the executive — not the monarch's appointment power; the distinction between the UK and Mexico is parliamentary vs. presidential system design, not judicial review.

choices.text: The UK is parliamentary — the PM can be removed by the legislature; Mexico is presidential — the executive serves a fixed term insulated from confidence votes.
choices.explanation: Per PAU-3.A.1 and PAU-3.A.2, the UK's parliamentary structure fuses executive and legislative authority — making the PM removable by no-confidence; Mexico's presidential system features separate election and fixed terms, preventing this mechanism.

choices.text: Mexico's bicameral legislature cannot pass no-confidence motions because its constitution bans legislative removal of the president.
choices.explanation: While correct that Mexico's constitution does not provide for a no-confidence vote, the explanation is incomplete; the deeper reason is systemic — Mexico's presidential design separates executive and legislative power, making no-confidence inappropriate as a mechanism.

choices.text: The UK's appointed House of Lords holds constitutional authority to veto any no-confidence motion passed by the elected House of Commons against the government.
choices.explanation: The House of Lords cannot veto a no-confidence motion passed by the Commons; no-confidence motions are Commons proceedings, and the Lords play no constitutional role in government formation or removal.

questions.subject: ap-comparative-government
questions.question: Which of the following conclusions is most directly supported by the data in the table?

choices.text: Countries with bicameral legislatures always exercise judicial review independently.
choices.explanation: Russia is bicameral yet the table marks it as exercising judicial review only formally, not independently; the relationship between bicameralism and judicial independence is not absolute.

choices.text: Unicameral legislatures are associated with the absence of independent judicial review in the course countries.
choices.explanation: Both countries with unicameral legislatures in the table — China and Iran — lack independent judicial review; while this is a pattern within the six course countries and not a universal law, the table's data directly supports this association.

choices.text: All course countries with appointed upper houses have fully independent judiciaries.
choices.explanation: Russia has an appointed Federation Council yet the table marks its judicial review as non-independent in practice; the UK, also with an appointed upper house, does have independent judicial review — the pattern is not uniform.

choices.text: The selection method for the upper house is the primary determinant of a country's degree of judicial independence.
choices.explanation: The table shows no consistent relationship between upper house selection method and judicial independence; judicial independence is shaped by appointment processes, tenure, and political context — not by whether the upper house is elected or appointed.

questions.subject: ap-comparative-government

stimulus.content: "Russia's constitution formally grants the State Duma the power to pass legislation and confirm the prime minister. However, critics note that the Duma consistently passes the president's proposals with minimal debate, opposition parties hold few seats, and the Federation Council — whose members are appointed by regional governors loyal to the Kremlin — ratifies decisions quickly. When the president dissolves the Duma, legislative power effectively reverts to presidential decrees."

questions.question: Which of the following best explains the gap between Russia's formal constitutional framework and its actual legislative practice?

choices.text: Russia's semi-presidential structure inherently prevents any legislative independence, making the formal powers of the Duma irrelevant.
choices.explanation: Semi-presidentialism does not inherently eliminate legislative independence — it is a structural category that can coexist with varying degrees of legislative autonomy; the passage points to political management, not an inherent structural impossibility.

choices.text: Russia's presidential dominance and Kremlin-aligned Federation Council appointments effectively bring both legislative chambers under executive control.
choices.explanation: Per PAU-3.E.1.e and PAU-3.G.1.g, Russia's formal legislative and judicial powers exist on paper but are undermined in practice by the president's political control over the Duma, the appointed Federation Council, and the broader managed-democracy apparatus.

choices.text: Russia's unicameral national legislature structurally lacks the institutional authority and capacity needed to effectively challenge an executive-dominant system.
choices.explanation: Russia has a bicameral legislature (Duma + Federation Council), not a unicameral one; the passage's analysis of both chambers underscores this.

choices.text: The Federation Council's role as an elected upper house provides executive checks on the Duma that prevent independent legislation.
choices.explanation: The passage explicitly states the Federation Council members are appointed by Kremlin-aligned governors — not elected; calling them elected misrepresents a key institutional fact about Russia's legislative structure.

questions.subject: ap-comparative-government

stimulus.content: "A scholar of comparative institutions argues: 'The degree of judicial independence is not captured simply by whether a constitution grants courts the power of judicial review. It depends equally on how judges are selected, how long they serve, what professional qualifications they require, and how difficult it is to remove them once appointed.'"

questions.question: Which of the following course countries best illustrates the argument that formal judicial review power and actual judicial independence can diverge?

choices.text: The United Kingdom, whose courts apply common law effectively but lack a formal written constitution to use as a basis for judicial review.
choices.explanation: The UK is the course example of effective judicial independence; the passage's argument concerns divergence between formal review power and real independence — the UK's case is the opposite of divergence.

choices.text: Mexico, whose Supreme Court has exercised judicial review and approved constitutional amendments to improve independence.
choices.explanation: Mexico's judiciary represents alignment rather than divergence — it has formal judicial review AND is making progress toward real independence; the passage's argument targets countries with formal power but no real independence.

choices.text: Russia, where the constitution formally grants judicial review but courts in practice are used to target opposition rather than constrain executive authority.
choices.explanation: Per PAU-3.G.1.g, Russia formally has judicial review but in practice the judiciary is used as a political tool against the opposition — precisely the divergence between formal power and actual independence the passage describes.

choices.text: Iran, whose judiciary enforces Sharia law and therefore exercises judicial independence from civil legislative authority.
choices.explanation: Iran's judiciary enforces Sharia through judges appointed by the Supreme Leader — it is structurally dependent on the theocratic apex of the system, not independent; Sharia enforcement is not the same as judicial independence from executive authority.

questions.subject: ap-comparative-government

stimulus.content: "A researcher compares two countries: In Country P, the president is the only executive leader, serves a fixed six-year term, cannot seek re-election, and is directly elected by the public. In Country Q, a popularly elected president nominates a prime minister, who must be confirmed by the lower house of the legislature. The prime minister oversees the civil service, while the president conducts foreign policy and commands the military."

questions.question: Which of the following correctly identifies the system types of Country P and Country Q?

choices.text: Country P is parliamentary; Country Q is presidential.
choices.explanation: Country P has a directly elected, fixed-term executive who is both head of state and head of government — the presidential model; Country Q has a separately elected president and a PM confirmed by the legislature — the semi-presidential model.

choices.text: Country P is presidential; Country Q is semi-presidential.
choices.explanation: Country P matches Mexico's presidential model (PAU-3.A.2): fixed term, direct election, single executive. Country Q matches Russia's semi-presidential model (PAU-3.A.3): elected president nominates a PM confirmed by the legislature, with divided executive functions.

choices.text: Country P is semi-presidential; Country Q is parliamentary.
choices.explanation: Country P lacks a prime minister entirely, ruling out semi-presidential; Country Q has both a president and a PM confirmed by the legislature — that is semi-presidential, not parliamentary.

choices.text: Both Country P and Country Q are presidential systems because both feature a directly elected executive.
choices.explanation: Having a directly elected president is not sufficient to classify a system as presidential; Country Q also has a prime minister confirmed by the legislature, making it semi-presidential — the dual accountability structure distinguishes it from a pure presidential system.

questions.subject: ap-comparative-government

stimulus.content: "The National People's Congress of China met for its annual session and unanimously approved the premier, ratified the national budget, and endorsed all proposed constitutional amendments. The session concluded in ten days. Observers noted that all major decisions had been announced by CPC leadership several weeks before the session convened."

questions.question: The passage best supports which of the following arguments about China's political institutions?

choices.text: The NPC's consistent near-unanimous votes on all measures demonstrate that China has successfully achieved broad political consensus and deep social harmony.
choices.explanation: Unanimity in the NPC reflects CPC control and the absence of genuine debate, not organic social consensus; attributing unanimous votes to 'social harmony' misrepresents the political mechanism at work.

choices.text: The NPC exercises independent legislative authority because the constitution recognizes it as the most powerful institution.
choices.explanation: This is the classic misconception identified in §5 of unit-2.md: the constitution's language does not reflect actual power; the NPC ratifies decisions made by the Politburo Standing Committee — formal constitutional status does not equal real authority.

choices.text: The NPC rubber-stamps Politburo Standing Committee decisions, lending formal legislative legitimacy to CPC choices without independent deliberation.
choices.explanation: Per PAU-3.E.1.a and PAU-3.F.1.a, the NPC meets briefly, approves pre-announced CPC decisions, and legitimizes policy without independent deliberation — the defining characteristics of a rubber-stamp legislature constrained by the Politburo Standing Committee.

choices.text: China's unicameral legislature is effective precisely because it avoids inter-chamber conflict that slows lawmaking in bicameral systems.
choices.explanation: The passage's point is about CPC control, not legislative efficiency; while China is unicameral, the speed and unanimity of the NPC reflects party dominance, not a structural advantage of unicameralism.

questions.subject: ap-comparative-government

stimulus.content: "In the United Kingdom, the House of Lords reviewed a bill that had been passed by the House of Commons. The Lords proposed amendments that the Commons ultimately rejected. By convention, the Lords deferred to the elected chamber and allowed the bill to proceed into law — though the process added several months to the timeline."

questions.question: Which of the following best describes the role of the House of Lords illustrated in the passage?

choices.text: The House of Lords serves as a veto player that can permanently block legislation passed by the House of Commons.
choices.explanation: Per PAU-3.E.1.f, the House of Lords can review and amend bills and delay implementation, but it cannot permanently block legislation passed by the Commons — the Commons ultimately has supremacy.

choices.text: The House of Lords functions as a revising chamber that can delay but not permanently block Commons legislation.
choices.explanation: The passage illustrates PAU-3.E.1.f precisely: the Lords reviewed, proposed amendments, and added time — but ultimately deferred, confirming that the Lords' power is delaying and revising, not vetoing.

choices.text: The House of Lords exercises independent judicial review, overturning legislation it finds unconstitutional.
choices.explanation: Judicial review in the UK is exercised by the UK Supreme Court, not the House of Lords; since the 2005 Constitutional Reform Act, the Lords is purely a legislative body and the Supreme Court handles judicial functions.

choices.text: The House of Lords is an elected chamber that represents regional interests as a check on the lower house.
choices.explanation: The House of Lords is appointed, not elected; per PAU-3.E.1.f, it is the appointed upper chamber whose power to check the Commons is limited to delay and revision.

questions.subject: ap-comparative-government
questions.question: Which of the following best describes the concept of 'rule by law' as applied to China's judicial system?

choices.text: All persons and institutions, including the government, are equally accountable to the law.
choices.explanation: Equal accountability of the government to law is the definition of rule of law — the opposite of rule by law; China's system does not hold the CPC equally accountable.

choices.text: The judicial system enforces religious law above civil statutes, as in Iran's Sharia-based courts.
choices.explanation: China's judicial system is based on civil law and CPC authority, not religious law; Sharia-based enforcement describes Iran's judiciary, not China's.

choices.text: The law is used as an instrument of state control rather than as a constraint on state power.
choices.explanation: Per PAU-3.G.1.a, rule by law in China means the CPC deploys the judicial system to achieve state goals — punishing dissent, enforcing compliance — rather than as a mechanism that limits the state itself.

choices.text: Courts exercise robust judicial review and regularly overturn legislation that contradicts the constitution.
choices.explanation: Robust independent judicial review characterizes rule of law, not rule by law; China's courts do not independently overturn CPC-supported legislation.

questions.subject: ap-comparative-government

stimulus.content: "In Country W, the Majles passed a bill to increase internet access in rural areas. Before the bill could take effect, a council of Islamic jurists reviewed the legislation and ruled that certain provisions conflicted with their interpretation of Islamic law. The council returned the bill to the Majles with mandatory revisions. The Majles revised the bill and resubmitted it; only after a second approval by the council did the law take effect."

questions.question: Country W's institutional process best illustrates which of the following concepts?

choices.text: Judicial review in which the Supreme Court independently overturns legislation on constitutional grounds.
choices.explanation: The body described reviews legislation for compliance with Islamic law, not a written constitution, and is part of the political-religious system rather than an independent judiciary; this is Guardian Council oversight, not independent judicial review.

choices.text: A legislative veto in which the upper house can permanently block legislation from the lower house.
choices.explanation: The council described is not an upper chamber; it is an appointed religious oversight body (the Guardian Council) that reviews legislation for Sharia compliance — a distinct function from upper-chamber legislative vetoes.

choices.text: Legislative independence constrained by a non-elected religious oversight body that enforces compliance with Islamic law.
choices.explanation: Per PAU-3.F.1.d, Iran's Guardian Council constrains Majles independence by vetting legislation for Sharia compliance and returning non-compliant bills for revision — exactly the process described in Country W.

choices.text: A bicameral conflict resolution mechanism in which an upper house mediates disputes between the lower house and the executive.
choices.explanation: Iran's Majles is unicameral; the process described is Guardian Council oversight, not inter-chamber mediation; dispute mediation between the Majles and Guardian Council is the Expediency Council's function.

questions.subject: ap-comparative-government

stimulus.content: "Mexico's Senate holds powers that the Chamber of Deputies does not. It has the authority to confirm presidential nominees to the Supreme Court, approve treaties between Mexico and foreign governments, and authorize federal intervention in state matters when constitutional order has broken down."

questions.question: The passage most directly illustrates which feature of Mexico's legislative system?

choices.text: The Chamber of Deputies and the Senate share identical powers under Mexico's unicameral constitution.
choices.explanation: Mexico has a bicameral congress, not a unicameral one; the passage explicitly describes powers unique to the Senate — confirming these chambers are constitutionally distinct.

choices.text: Mexico's bicameral Congress divides functions: the Senate handles judicial appointments and treaties; the Chamber of Deputies handles fiscal and electoral matters.
choices.explanation: Per PAU-3.E.1.c, Mexico's Senate uniquely holds powers to confirm Supreme Court appointments, approve treaties, and authorize federal intervention — illustrating the distinct roles in Mexico's bicameral system.

choices.text: Mexico's Senate exercises the power to impeach the president with a simple majority vote.
choices.explanation: Impeachment in Mexico's presidential system would involve both chambers; the passage does not describe impeachment power, and the CED notes executive removal is extremely rare in Mexico.

choices.text: Mexico's Senate can veto any legislation passed by the Chamber of Deputies, creating a recurring gridlock mechanism between the two chambers.
choices.explanation: The passage describes specific unique powers of the Senate — not a general veto over lower-house legislation; Mexico's bicameral system requires both chambers to pass legislation, but the passage focuses on the Senate's distinctive functional roles.

questions.subject: ap-comparative-government

stimulus.content: "A political scientist studying Africa argues: 'Nigeria's National Assembly has the formal constitutional power to impeach the president by a two-thirds supermajority in both chambers, yet this mechanism has never been fully invoked to remove a sitting president. The high threshold, the need for bipartisan consensus, and the political costs of impeachment make it a theoretical check that constrains executive behavior through its existence rather than its frequent use.'"

questions.question: The scholar's argument most directly supports which of the following claims about Nigeria's political institutions?

choices.text: Because impeachment has never been used, the Nigerian National Assembly holds no meaningful legislative power.
choices.explanation: The absence of completed impeachments does not imply legislative impotence; the scholar explicitly argues the threat of impeachment functions as a deterrent — constraining executive behavior even without being invoked.

choices.text: The existence of a formal impeachment power can constrain executive behavior even if it is rarely or never fully exercised.
choices.explanation: The scholar's argument illustrates PAU-3.D.1 and PAU-3.F.2: formal legislative powers can reinforce accountability and constrain the executive through the credible threat of their use, independent of how often they are actually deployed.

choices.text: Nigeria's presidential system is functionally identical to a parliamentary system because the legislature can remove the executive.
choices.explanation: Impeachment and a vote of no confidence are fundamentally different mechanisms; Nigeria's two-thirds supermajority impeachment for gross misconduct is far more difficult to invoke than a parliamentary no-confidence vote, maintaining the presidential character of the system.

choices.text: Nigeria's constitution should lower the impeachment threshold to make the legislature more effective.
choices.explanation: The scholar describes the institutional logic of the high threshold — deterrence — not a policy recommendation to lower it; introducing a normative claim about what the constitution 'should' do goes beyond the passage.

questions.subject: ap-comparative-government

stimulus.content: "An editorial in a political journal argues: 'A country may claim its courts have the power of judicial review, but the true test of an independent judiciary is whether judges will uphold a ruling that the executive opposes. Countries where judges serve brief, renewable terms — appointed by the sitting president — are unlikely to produce genuinely independent rulings, regardless of constitutional language.'"

questions.question: Which of the following best evaluates the editorial's argument using evidence from the course countries?

choices.text: The argument is undermined by Nigeria, whose president appoints Supreme Court judges but whose judiciary has shown increasing independence.
choices.explanation: Nigeria actually supports the nuanced version of the argument: presidential appointment without sufficient independence safeguards can threaten judicial independence; Nigeria's judiciary is noted as working to reestablish independence — not a counterexample to the argument.

choices.text: Mexico supports the argument: 15-year non-renewable terms insulate magistrates from the president who appointed them.
choices.explanation: Per PAU-3.G.1.d and PAU-3.H.1, Mexico's 15-year non-renewable term for magistrates directly addresses the editorial's concern — long, non-renewable terms insulate judges from political pressure regardless of the appointment source.

choices.text: The argument is refuted by Russia, which demonstrates that presidential appointment of judges always produces independent judicial outcomes.
choices.explanation: Russia directly supports the editorial's argument: Russian judges are nominated by the president and confirmed by the Federation Council, and the judiciary consistently sides with the executive — precisely the dynamic the editorial predicts.

choices.text: The argument ignores Iran, where the judiciary's independence from the elected president is absolute because judges are appointed by the Supreme Leader.
choices.explanation: Iran's judiciary being independent of the elected president does not mean it is independent in the meaningful sense — it is fully subordinate to the Supreme Leader; the editorial's point concerns independence from political pressure broadly, which Iran's judiciary clearly lacks.

questions.subject: ap-comparative-government

stimulus.content: "A legislator in Country V argues: 'Our system is superior because the government must continuously earn the trust of this legislature. If the prime minister loses our confidence, we remove the cabinet and call new elections. There are no fixed terms that protect an unpopular government from accountability.'"

questions.question: A critic of the legislator's argument would most likely point out which of the following potential weaknesses of the system described?

choices.text: The system described does not allow the legislature to question the executive through formal interrogation sessions.
choices.explanation: Parliamentary systems feature robust question time and committee oversight; the absence of formal interrogation is not a recognized weakness of parliamentary accountability mechanisms.

choices.text: Ongoing confidence requirements mean coalition governments can collapse over policy disputes, creating instability that disrupts long-term policy implementation.
choices.explanation: Per PAU-3.B.1, while parliamentary systems have fewer institutional obstacles to enact policy, they can suffer from instability — coalition governments may fall over policy disputes, and the lack of fixed terms can create uncertainty that impedes long-term policy continuity.

choices.text: The parliamentary system completely prevents any judicial oversight of legislation, since the legislature's supreme authority places it above review by the courts.
choices.explanation: Parliamentary supremacy in the UK tradition does limit the scope of judicial review of legislation, but this is not the primary weakness the legislator's argument invites; the critic would more likely focus on stability, not judicial oversight.

choices.text: The system is inferior because it allows the executive to remain in power without winning a popular election.
choices.explanation: In parliamentary systems the PM is indirectly accountable to voters through the legislature; the system's potential weakness is instability, not democratic deficit — parliamentary executives are accountable, just differently than directly elected executives.

questions.subject: ap-comparative-government

stimulus.content: "In northern Nigeria, state governments have established courts that adjudicate criminal and civil matters according to Islamic Sharia law. These courts operate alongside Nigeria's federal court system. Critics argue that the existence of parallel court systems creates unequal legal treatment for Nigerians in different regions; supporters argue it reflects the federal structure and cultural autonomy of Muslim-majority northern states."

questions.question: The passage best illustrates which of the following features of Nigeria's judicial system?

choices.text: Nigeria's federalism permits Sharia courts to operate in northern states, creating a dual judicial track alongside the national court system.
choices.explanation: Per PAU-3.G.1.e, under Nigeria's federalism Islamic Sharia courts have been established in the north — the passage describes this dual judicial track, illustrating how federalism shapes the judicial landscape at the subnational level.

choices.text: Nigeria operates a unitary judicial system that applies a single, uniform body of law consistently across all thirty-six states and the capital territory.
choices.explanation: The passage explicitly describes parallel court systems operating in different regions — evidence of judicial diversity, not uniformity; Nigeria is a federal state with subnational judicial variation.

choices.text: Nigeria's Supreme Court has ruled Sharia courts unconstitutional, demonstrating robust judicial review.
choices.explanation: The CED does not record a Supreme Court ruling eliminating Sharia courts; per PAU-3.G.1.e, Sharia courts exist under Nigeria's federalism and the passage presents them as operational and contested, not ruled unconstitutional.

choices.text: Nigeria's judicial system is identical to Iran's because both countries apply Sharia law in their courts.
choices.explanation: Iran's entire judicial system is constitutionally based on Sharia; Nigeria applies Sharia only in selected northern states under a federal framework, alongside a secular national court system — the scope and constitutional basis are fundamentally different.

questions.subject: ap-comparative-government

stimulus.content: "China's president concurrently holds three titles: head of state (President of the People's Republic), leader of the party (General Secretary of the Chinese Communist Party), and head of the military (Chair of the Central Military Commission). The premier, who is nominated by the president and confirmed by the National People's Congress, manages the civil service and chairs the State Council."

questions.question: Which of the following best explains the significance of the institutional arrangement described in the passage?

choices.text: The arrangement demonstrates that China operates as a presidential system identical to Mexico's, since one person holds the top executive position.
choices.explanation: China's system is a one-party state, not a competitive presidential system; the basis of authority, accountability mechanisms, and legitimacy differ fundamentally from Mexico's — one person holding multiple roles in a one-party context is categorically distinct.

choices.text: Consolidating party, state, and military authority in one leader eliminates meaningful separation of powers at the apex of China's system.
choices.explanation: Per PAU-3.C.2.a, China's president combines the CPC General Secretary, state presidency, and Central Military Commission chair — fusing party, state, and military authority in a single figure; changes in top leadership occur through internal party mechanisms, not transparent electoral processes.

choices.text: The role of premier as head of government provides an independent check on the president's power because the NPC must confirm the premier.
choices.explanation: The NPC confirmation is formal rather than substantive — the NPC is a rubber-stamp body; the premier is nominated by the president and in practice operates as an executive subordinate, not an independent check.

choices.text: China's dual executive structure is identical to Russia's because both systems have a president and a head of government (premier or prime minister).
choices.explanation: Russia's dual executive involves a separately popularly elected president and a PM accountable to the Duma in a competitive semi-presidential system; China's premier is a CPC-controlled subordinate with no independent electoral mandate — the structural logic differs fundamentally.

questions.subject: ap-comparative-government
questions.question: Which of the following correctly identifies how the United Kingdom's prime minister can be removed from office?

choices.text: By a two-thirds supermajority impeachment vote in the House of Commons following a formal investigation into gross misconduct.
choices.explanation: Impeachment of the PM is not the standard removal mechanism in the UK; the primary mechanism is a vote of no confidence by a simple majority of the House of Commons.

choices.text: By a Commons no-confidence vote or by the ruling party replacing the PM as its party leader.
choices.explanation: Per PAU-3.D.1, the UK PM can be removed by a House of Commons no-confidence vote or by losing the party leadership — both of which have occurred historically (e.g., Thatcher resigned after losing internal party support).

choices.text: By a decree from the monarch dissolving the government and appointing a new PM without a parliamentary vote.
choices.explanation: The monarch acts on convention and ministerial advice; unilateral royal decrees removing PMs do not occur in modern UK constitutional practice — the Commons or the governing party initiates executive change.

choices.text: By a referendum in which the public votes directly to remove the PM before the scheduled election.
choices.explanation: The UK has no constitutional mechanism for a public recall referendum targeting the PM; removal is a parliamentary process, not a direct-democracy mechanism.

questions.subject: ap-comparative-government

stimulus.content: "A comparative politics textbook states: 'Independent legislatures serve functions beyond lawmaking. By publicly debating policy proposals, facilitating compromise among competing interests, and responding to constituent demands, legislatures can build public trust in the political system. A legislature that merely ratifies executive decisions loses this legitimating function over time.'"

questions.question: Which of the following course countries' legislatures best exemplifies the 'legitimating function' described in the passage?

choices.text: China's National People's Congress, which meets annually to ratify decisions made by the Politburo Standing Committee.
choices.explanation: The NPC is precisely the type of rubber-stamp body the passage says loses its legitimating function — it does not publicly debate policy independently or respond to constituent demands.

choices.text: Iran's Majles, which approves legislation and conducts policy debates, albeit under the constraining oversight of the Guardian Council.
choices.explanation: While the Majles holds some genuine debates, the Guardian Council's oversight severely constrains independent policymaking; Iran's legislature partially but not fully exemplifies the passage's model of independent legitimation.

choices.text: The UK's House of Commons, which openly debates legislation, questions the government publicly, and maintains direct accountability to the electorate.
choices.explanation: Per PAU-3.F.2, independent legislatures reinforce legitimacy by openly debating policy and responding to public demand; the House of Commons exemplifies this with robust question time, committee scrutiny, and electoral accountability.

choices.text: Russia's State Duma, which confirms the prime minister and has passed legislation with broad cross-party support.
Russia's State Duma, which confirms the prime minister and passes legislation with cross-party endorsement.
choices.explanation: Russia's Duma is dominated by parties aligned with the president and does not independently respond to public demand or facilitate genuine policy compromise; broad support in this context reflects managed politics, not independent legitimation.

questions.subject: ap-comparative-government
questions.question: Which of the following correctly identifies the body in Iran that can select and remove the Supreme Leader?

choices.text: The Guardian Council
choices.explanation: The Guardian Council vets candidates for elected office and reviews legislation for Sharia compliance; it does not have the power to select or remove the Supreme Leader.

choices.text: The Expediency Council
choices.explanation: The Expediency Council resolves disputes between the Majles and the Guardian Council; it advises the Supreme Leader but has no authority to select or remove the Supreme Leader.

choices.text: The Majles (Parliament)
choices.explanation: The Majles is Iran's elected parliament with powers over legislation, the budget, and cabinet confirmation; it does not hold the power to remove the Supreme Leader.

choices.text: Assembly of Experts
choices.explanation: Per PAU-3.D.1 (Unit 2 cross-reference), the Assembly of Experts — composed of Islamic scholars — is the body that selects, oversees, and holds the formal power to remove Iran's Supreme Leader.

questions.subject: ap-comparative-government

stimulus.content: "A scholar writes: 'When voters elect a president who then serves a single non-renewable term, the executive is freed from constant electoral calculations. This can enable bold, long-horizon policy decisions. Yet the same dynamic means that a capable, reform-minded leader must abandon office precisely at the moment when the results of their reforms may be starting to bear fruit.'"

questions.question: The scholar's argument most directly illustrates which tension regarding executive term limits?

choices.text: Term limits on the executive make it substantially more difficult for any country to develop and sustain a fully independent judiciary.
choices.explanation: The passage addresses executive tenure and policy continuity, not the relationship between term limits and judicial independence.

choices.text: Term limits prevent the development of democratic institutions by reducing the executive's time in office.
choices.explanation: The passage does not claim term limits harm democratic institutions broadly; it describes a specific trade-off between freedom from electoral calculations and insufficient time to complete reforms.

choices.text: Freedom from re-election pressure is both an advantage (bolder policy) and a disadvantage (insufficient time to achieve goals).
choices.explanation: Per PAU-3.C.3, the CED lists focusing on governing rather than winning elections as an advantage of term limits, while insufficient time to achieve goals is listed as a disadvantage; the scholar captures precisely this dual nature.

choices.text: Term limits are disadvantageous in presidential systems but beneficial in parliamentary systems.
choices.explanation: The CED presents term limits as having advantages and disadvantages without distinguishing between system types; parliamentary systems typically do not use fixed term limits for the executive in the same way.

questions.subject: ap-comparative-government

stimulus.content: "Mexico's Chamber of Deputies consists of 500 members elected through a mixed system, and holds the exclusive power to approve the national budget and verify the outcome of federal elections. The Senate has 128 members and holds the unique power to approve presidential nominations to the Supreme Court and to authorize federal intervention in states."

questions.question: Which of the following best describes the relationship between Mexico's Chamber of Deputies and Senate illustrated in the passage?

choices.text: Mexico's legislature is unicameral, with the Chamber of Deputies and Senate acting as subcommittees of the same body.
choices.explanation: The passage describes two chambers with distinct memberships and distinct constitutional powers — confirming Mexico's bicameral structure per PAU-3.E.1.c.

choices.text: Both the Chamber of Deputies and the Senate hold identical constitutional powers, including approving legislation and confirming all judicial appointments.
choices.explanation: The passage explicitly shows different powers: the Chamber of Deputies approves the budget and verifies elections; the Senate confirms Supreme Court nominations and approves federal intervention — they are not identical.

choices.text: Mexico's bicameral legislature assigns distinct constitutional powers to each chamber, so each holds exclusive functions the other cannot exercise.
choices.explanation: Per PAU-3.E.1.c, Mexico's bicameral system assigns distinct powers to each chamber: the Chamber of Deputies handles budget and election verification; the Senate handles judicial confirmation and treaty approval — a functional division the passage illustrates.

choices.text: The Senate holds more power than the Chamber of Deputies because its members are directly elected.
choices.explanation: Both the Senate and the Chamber of Deputies are elected; the passage does not indicate one chamber holds superior overall authority — they hold different specific powers within a co-equal bicameral framework.

questions.subject: ap-comparative-government

stimulus.content: "A political scientist compares the UK and Russia: 'Both systems have a prime minister, but the analogy ends there. In the United Kingdom, the prime minister is the dominant executive who governs by maintaining Commons support and can only be removed by losing a confidence vote or party leadership. In Russia, the prime minister is a subordinate official whose position exists at the pleasure of the directly elected president — who may dismiss the PM without a parliamentary vote.'"

questions.question: Which of the following most directly addresses the misconception that the UK and Russia have similar systems because both have a prime minister?

choices.text: Russia and the UK have the same system because both have elected executives who serve fixed terms.
choices.explanation: The UK PM does not serve a fixed term and is not directly elected; this choice conflates the systems rather than addressing the misconception.

choices.text: The UK is parliamentary — the PM is the top executive and is accountable to the Commons; Russia is semi-presidential — the PM is subordinate to the dominant elected president.
choices.explanation: Per PAU-3.A.1, PAU-3.A.3, and §5 misconception #5 of unit-2.md, the key distinction is that the UK's PM holds the top executive authority and is drawn from Parliament, while Russia's PM is subordinate to the directly elected president — the two systems are structurally distinct despite the shared title.

choices.text: Both the UK and Russia are semi-presidential because each features a head of government (PM) alongside a separate ceremonial head of state (a monarch or elected president).
choices.explanation: The UK has a ceremonial monarch as head of state and an executive PM as head of government — this is the parliamentary model, not semi-presidential; Russia's president is executive, not ceremonial.

choices.text: The presence of a prime minister proves that both countries have fused legislative and executive powers.
choices.explanation: Fused powers is a feature of parliamentary systems (UK) but not semi-presidential systems (Russia); Russia's president and PM are both executive officials, but the president is separately elected — there is no fusion of executive and legislative authority.

questions.subject: ap-comparative-government

stimulus.content: "A student argues: 'Iran's elected president is the most powerful figure in the country because voters directly choose the president, who then manages the civil service and conducts foreign policy. The Supreme Leader, while powerful, is unelected and therefore lacks true democratic legitimacy.'"

questions.question: Which of the following best evaluates the student's argument?

choices.text: The argument is correct because democratic legitimacy through direct elections is always the primary source of political power.
choices.explanation: Democratic legitimacy through elections is one source of political power, but it is not universal; Iran's system is explicitly designed so that theocratic authority — not electoral legitimacy — is the supreme source of power.

choices.text: The argument is flawed: the Supreme Leader commands the military, appoints the judiciary, and controls candidate vetting — making him far more powerful than the elected president.
choices.explanation: Per PAU-3.C.2.b and §5 misconception #3 of unit-2.md, Iran's Supreme Leader holds authority far exceeding the elected president's: he commands the military, appoints the judiciary, oversees the Guardian Council, and sets the political agenda — making popular election a misleading proxy for political power in Iran.

choices.text: The argument is partially correct because the elected president and the Supreme Leader hold exactly equal authority in Iran's dual executive.
choices.explanation: Iran's dual executive is explicitly hierarchical, not equal; the Supreme Leader holds supreme authority above the elected president — calling them equal misrepresents the constitutional and practical power relationship.

choices.text: The argument is correct because, despite holding the title of Supreme Leader, this official's powers are largely ceremonial and symbolic in actual practice.
choices.explanation: The Supreme Leader's powers are far from ceremonial — commanding the military, appointing the head of the judiciary, and overseeing candidate vetting are substantive political functions that shape all major policy outcomes.

questions.subject: ap-comparative-government

stimulus.content: "Nigeria's president is directly elected by the public and serves as both head of state and head of government. The president commands the armed forces, oversees the civil service, can approve domestic legislation, and conducts foreign policy. Cabinet members are appointed by and accountable to the president."

questions.question: Which of the following best explains why Nigeria's system is classified as presidential rather than parliamentary?

choices.text: Nigeria operates a unicameral national legislature, and this single-chamber structure is a defining constitutional feature of all presidential systems.
choices.explanation: Nigeria's National Assembly is bicameral (Senate and House of Representatives); unicameralism is not a defining feature of presidential systems — and Nigeria is bicameral.

choices.text: Nigeria's directly elected executive serves a fixed term and is protected from removal by a simple legislative confidence vote.
choices.explanation: Per PAU-3.A.2, presidential systems feature separate fixed-term elections for the executive and a legislature that can only remove cabinet members through impeachment — not through a confidence vote; Nigeria's system matches all these criteria.

choices.text: Nigeria's president is appointed by the legislature rather than directly elected by citizens.
choices.explanation: Nigeria's president is directly elected by citizens in a popular vote — not appointed by the legislature; legislative appointment of the executive is characteristic of parliamentary systems, not presidential ones.

choices.text: Nigeria has a prime minister who serves as head of government separate from the president as head of state.
choices.explanation: Nigeria has no prime minister; the president serves as both head of state and head of government — a presidential-system feature explicitly identified in PAU-3.A.2 and described in the passage.

questions.subject: ap-comparative-government

rows: Majles can impeach by simple majority
NPC formally; CPC controls in practice

questions.question: Which of the following conclusions is best supported by the data in the table?

choices.text: Countries with fixed presidential terms always have stricter term limits than countries with parliamentary systems.
choices.explanation: The table shows China's president has no formal term limit (after 2018 amendment) yet has a fixed 5-year term; the UK PM has no fixed term but also no term limit — the relationship between fixed terms and strict term limits is not uniform.

choices.text: Parliamentary executives serve without fixed terms — removal happens through a confidence vote rather than waiting for a fixed term to end.
choices.explanation: The UK row confirms this: no fixed term, removal by confidence vote — giving the legislature direct, ongoing control over executive tenure; presidential and semi-presidential systems rely on fixed terms with limited removal mechanisms.

choices.text: Nigeria and Iran have identical presidential systems because both have 4-year terms and allow legislative impeachment.
choices.explanation: Nigeria and Iran share some structural features, but Iran's president operates within a theocratic system under the Supreme Leader's authority — making the systems fundamentally different despite similar term structures.

choices.text: China's constitutional removal of formal presidential term limits in 2018 means that China must now be reclassified as a presidential system.
choices.explanation: System classification (parliamentary, presidential, semi-presidential) is determined by executive-legislative relations and selection mechanisms, not by whether term limits exist; China remains a one-party state regardless of the 2018 amendment.

questions.subject: ap-comparative-government

stimulus.content: "The Standing Committee of the National People's Congress meets throughout the year to carry out legislative functions when the full NPC is not in session. It sets the NPC's legislative agenda, interprets the constitution and laws, supervises NPC member elections, and can pass certain legislative measures that the full Congress later ratifies."

questions.question: Which of the following best explains the significance of the Standing Committee of the NPC described in the passage?

choices.text: It demonstrates that China has an independent judiciary because the Standing Committee can interpret the constitution.
